title: Using AI Agents Effectively
description: Learn to work with AI agents as powerful tools — understanding their capabilities, limitations, and how to get the best results.

lessons.title: AI Agents vs. Chatbots vs. Automation
objectives: Distinguish between a chatbot, an AI agent, and traditional automation
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: Think of it like cooking. A chatbot is like a recipe card — it gives you fixed answers. Automation is like a rice cooker — it does one thing reliably. An AI agent is like having a sous-chef — it understands your goal, makes decisions, uses tools, and adapts when things change.
lessons.commonMistake: Assuming all AI tools are agents. Most chatbots are not agents — they don't take actions or use tools.

lessons.title: How AI Agents Work Under the Hood
objectives: Understand the basic loop: perceive, reason, act
Know what 'tools' and 'context' mean in agent terms
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: An AI agent works in a cycle: (1) it reads your request and any context, (2) it thinks about what to do, (3) it takes an action (like reading a file or searching the web), (4) it observes the result, and (5) it decides what to do next. This loop repeats until the task is done.
lessons.commonMistake: Thinking the agent 'remembers' everything. Agents have limited context windows — they work with what's in front of them.

lessons.title: Writing Clear Instructions
objectives: Write prompts that give agents enough context to act
Understand why vague prompts produce vague results
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: Imagine asking a new employee to 'fix the thing.' They'd be lost. But if you say 'The login button on the settings page returns a 404 error — can you check the route handler in auth.py?', they know exactly where to look. AI agents work the same way.
lessons.commonMistake: Being too vague ('make it better') or too prescriptive ('do exactly these 47 steps'). The sweet spot is clear intent with enough context.

lessons.title: What to Avoid When Prompting
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: Common anti-patterns: (1) Prompt stuffing — cramming unrelated requests into one message. (2) Implicit assumptions — expecting the agent to know your codebase without showing it. (3) Moving goalposts — changing requirements mid-task without acknowledging the shift.
lessons.commonMistake: Blaming the agent for bad output when the prompt was unclear. Always check your prompt first.

lessons.title: Understanding What Tools an Agent Has
objectives: Identify common agent tools (file read/write, search, web, shell)
Know how to ask an agent what it can do
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: An agent's tools are like a carpenter's toolbox. A carpenter with only a hammer will try to hammer everything. Knowing what tools your agent has helps you give it tasks it can actually accomplish.
lessons.commonMistake: Asking an agent to do something its tools don't support, then getting frustrated when it fails or hallucinates.

lessons.title: Reviewing and Verifying Agent Work
objectives: Develop habits for checking agent output
Know when to trust and when to verify
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: Trust, but verify. An agent might write code that looks correct but has a subtle bug, or give you information that's outdated. Always: (1) read the changes before accepting, (2) run tests, (3) check edge cases the agent might have missed.
lessons.commonMistake: Blindly accepting all agent output without review. The agent is a collaborator, not an oracle.

lessons.title: Multi-Step Workflows and Agent Planning
objectives: Guide an agent through complex, multi-step tasks
Know when to break tasks down vs. let the agent plan
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: For complex tasks, you have a choice: give the agent the full plan, or let it plan itself. Generally, give high-level goals and let the agent decompose — but verify its plan before it executes. Think of it as delegation: you set the direction, the agent figures out the steps.
lessons.commonMistake: Micromanaging every step (wastes the agent's strengths) or giving zero direction on complex tasks (leads to misaligned output).

lessons.title: Knowing When NOT to Use an Agent
objectives: Identify tasks better suited for direct action or simpler tools
lessons.keyConceptExplanation: Not every task needs an agent. If you can do it in 30 seconds with a script or a search, an agent adds overhead. Agents shine on tasks that require judgment, multi-step reasoning, or working across multiple tools simultaneously.
lessons.commonMistake: Using an agent for everything, including tasks where a simple grep or manual edit is faster.
